titleInfo.title: Randeng qifu hu jiyue—Xi’an beilin cang sheng Tang Fojiao ‘randeng shitai zan’ yishu xinzhi
燃燈祈福胡伎樂──西安碑林藏盛唐佛教‘燃燈石臺贊’藝術新知
Foreign Music for Lamp-Lighting Prayers—New Insights Into the Art of Buddhist ‘Stone Base Hymns of Lamp-Lighting’ of the High Tang Kept in the Forest of Steles in Xi’an

mods.abstract:
Lamp-lighting prayers are common rites of religious worship (Buddhism, Daoism, Zoroastrianism, Manichaeism) since the Northern Qi dynasty. In the 1950ies, a “lamp-lighting stone stand” 燃燈石臺 entered the collection of the Forest of Steles. It shows foreign musicians. Octagonal shaft, 1.2 m high. The original base and top are lost. Place of origin unknown. The title of the carved text is 燃燈石臺讚並序 , written by Li Xian 利憲, Prince of Ning 寧王. Transcription of the text given in simplified characters. The text names two palace officials as donors (府使蘇公, i.e. Su Sixu 蘇思勖, who died in 745, and Magistrate Tang 判官唐公). It is dated 大唐開元嵗次辛巳月直大火八日康申建 . 開元嵗次辛巳 is kaiyuan 29 (July 25, 741, if 月直大火 is the sixth month). After the date, the names and occupational titles of 66 male musicians are given, followed by a dedication to the emperor, empress, the crown prince, the imperial princes, all military and civil officials, beings reborn in the six paths and all the people (兆庶). The titles and names of these 66 persons reveal that this is a heterogeneous group with members who had access to various parts of the palace, probably members of some kind of an association 社, maybe a lamp-lighting association 燃燈社. The author lists 8 stone lanterns (without the Azure Dragon lantern) and analyses in more detail the Langfang 廊坊 stone pillar (dated 688, the lantern on top is gone missing), which also features dancers and musicians. The eternal light pillar from Langfang has 16 niches with Buddha figures in 2 rows on the upper part of the shaft, and the lower part of the octagonal shaft features 6 figures in shallow relief dancing and making music. Then the author gives an excursion of what is known from manuscripts about Dunhuang Mogao ku Lantern festivals (with dance and music, P.3405, P.3461). At Dunhuang, there is also influence from Sogdian immigrants 粟特移民 who originally believed in Zoroastrianism 祆教.
The final part of the article is about the style of the pictorial representations on the Forest of Steles’ "lamp-lighting stone pillar." They are influenced by the art of the Eastern Roman empire (拂菻), which was transmitted by sketches/stencils 粉本. This influence is also documented in the Painting Catalog of the Xuanhe Era (Xuanhe huapu 宣和畫譜). The Forest of Steles has some steles from the kaiyuan era that clearly show this influence from the Eastern Roman empire, often in the depiction of lions. The author lists more examples for this influence, mostly beasts, whose depiction received influences also from legends circulating in India, Iran, and among the Sogdians. The lamp-lighting stone pillar under study features in its bottom part foreigners 胡人 making music and winged beasts 神靈動物. The tomb of donor Su Sixu 蘇思勖, who died in 745, was excavated in 1952. It featured an illustration of an orchestra and dancers. The tomb of Li Xian 利憲 also has scenes of music and dance 樂舞圖 on the east wall.
